title: symphony: parallel agent swarm with hypothesis exploration and dead-end propagation
description:
## Motivation

Inspired by multi-agent orchestration patterns achieving 134x speedups through parallel hypothesis exploration (10 agents in isolated worktrees, communicating findings, dead ends propagated instantly, convergence on proven optima).

## Current State

- Symphony orchestrator supports max 2 concurrent agents (`max_concurrent_agents`)
- Agents work independently on Linear issues — no inter-agent communication channel
- No hypothesis tracking or dead-end propagation mechanism
- No convergence detection for optimization-focused swarms

## Proposed Changes

1. **Raise swarm ceiling**: configurable pool size up to 10+ concurrent agents via `MOLT_SYMPHONY_MAX_SWARM_SIZE`
2. **Shared hypothesis ledger**: add `SwarmHypothesisStore` to durable memory — agents register hypotheses (approach, expected gain, status) and query others' findings before starting work
3. **Dead-end broadcast**: when an agent proves an approach is suboptimal, it writes a `dead_end` event to the ledger; other agents' `before_run` hooks check the ledger and skip already-disproven approaches
4. **Convergence detector**: orchestrator monitors hypothesis ledger — when N agents converge on the same optimal solution, it stops remaining explorers and promotes the winner
5. **Isolated worktree per agent**: each swarm member gets a git worktree (already supported in workspace.py) for safe parallel code exploration

## TODO(symphony, owner:runtime, milestone:M2, priority:P1, status:planned): implement swarm hypothesis ledger and dead-end propagation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] 10 concurrent agents running without resource exhaustion
- [ ] Dead-end propagation latency < 5s
- [ ] Convergence detection triggers within 2 turns of agreement
- [ ] Durable memory tracks full hypothesis tree for post-hoc analysis

title: compiler: DAG-based optimization pass scheduler with cost-driven ordering
description:
## Motivation

DAG-based scheduling broke through a 6-iteration plateau in kernel optimization. Current Molt mid-end runs passes in a fixed order (canonicalize → SCCP → edge-thread → guard-hoist → CSE → DCE → region-simplify). A DAG scheduler could explore pass orderings per-function and find optimal sequences.

## Current State

- Fixed-order pass pipeline in `src/molt/frontend/__init__.py`
- Per-function wall-time budgets and tier classification (A/B/C)
- No feedback from pass effectiveness to ordering decisions
- Fixed-point loop retries the same sequence until convergence

## Proposed Changes

1. **Pass dependency DAG**: model each optimization pass as a DAG node with declared read/write effects on IR properties (constants, dead code, common subexprs, control flow)
2. **Cost estimator**: after each pass application, record (time_ms, ir_reduction_ratio, new_opportunities_created)
3. **Adaptive reordering**: use per-function pass effectiveness history to reorder the DAG for subsequent fixed-point iterations
4. **Parallelizable passes**: passes with non-overlapping effects (e.g., CSE on different function scopes) can run concurrently
5. **Configuration sweep mode**: `--sweep-pass-order` flag to exhaustively test orderings on a benchmark suite and emit optimal schedule

## TODO(compiler, owner:compiler, milestone:M2, priority:P1, status:planned): implement DAG-based pass scheduler with adaptive reordering

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Pass DAG models all 7+ mid-end passes with declared effects
- [ ] Adaptive reordering shows measurable improvement on at least 3 benchmarks
- [ ] Configuration sweep mode can test 1000+ orderings in <10 minutes
- [ ] No correctness regressions (formal Lean proof of pass commutativity where claimed)

title: runtime: merged hash stages for dict/set operations (algebraic hash merge)
description:
## Motivation

Merged hash stages was a key algorithmic insight in achieving 134x speedup — combining multiple hash computation passes into a single traversal. Current Molt runtime computes string hash via SIMD scan (ASCII check) + FNV hash as separate stages.

## Current State

- `simd_max_byte_value` scans bytes for ASCII check (separate pass)
- `hash_string_bytes` computes FNV hash (second pass over same data)
- Dict/set lookups: hash → probe → compare (3 stages, each touching memory)
- `dict[str] += int` prehash lane has deopt path (20K hits in taq.py benchmark)

## Proposed Changes

1. **Fused ASCII-hash kernel**: single SIMD pass that simultaneously checks ASCII-ness AND accumulates hash bits — eliminate the two-pass pattern
2. **Prehash+probe merge for dict[str]**: for string-keyed dicts, merge hash computation with probe sequence start (speculative first-slot check during hash finalization)
3. **Batch hash for dict construction**: when building dicts from known key sets (e.g., `{k: v for k, v in items}`), compute all key hashes in a single vectorized pass
4. **Hash algebraic properties**: exploit that FNV is distributive over XOR for set operations — merge `hash(a) ^ hash(b)` into single-pass `hash_pair(a, b)`

## TODO(runtime, owner:runtime, milestone:M2, priority:P1, status:planned): fuse ASCII-check and hash-compute into single SIMD pass

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Single-pass ASCII+hash kernel benchmarks faster than two-pass on strings >64 bytes
- [ ] dict[str] prehash deopt rate drops by >50% on taq.py benchmark
- [ ] No hash collision rate regression vs current FNV
- [ ] WASM path also benefits (non-SIMD fallback still fused)

title: compiler: path-bits scheduling for Cranelift instruction emission
description:
## Motivation

Path-bits scheduling was identified as a key technique for breaking optimization plateaus — encoding control-flow path information into scheduling decisions to avoid redundant work on cold paths and prioritize hot-path emission.

## Current State

- Cranelift codegen marks 36+ slow paths as cold blocks
- CFG edge frequencies computed but not used for emission ordering
- No path-sensitive instruction scheduling (all blocks scheduled uniformly)
- Register allocation uses linear scan (release) or single-pass (dev)

## Proposed Changes

1. **Path-bit annotations**: encode reachability bitmask per basic block — which entry paths reach this block
2. **Hot-path-first emission**: Cranelift block layout ordered by path-bit density (blocks reachable from more hot paths emitted first for i-cache locality)
3. **Path-sensitive register hints**: for values live across path splits, prefer registers that avoid spills on the hot path (inform Cranelift regalloc)
4. **Dead-path elision**: if path-bits prove a block is unreachable given type guards, elide it entirely from codegen (beyond DCE — this is codegen-level)
5. **Profile-guided path-bits**: feed runtime execution counts back to compiler to refine path-bit weights

## TODO(compiler, owner:compiler, milestone:M2, priority:P2, status:planned): implement path-bit block layout and hot-path emission ordering

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Hot-path blocks within same cache line in >80% of generated functions
- [ ] Measurable i-cache miss reduction on sum_list and word_count benchmarks
- [ ] Path-sensitive register hints reduce spill count by >10% on complex functions
- [ ] Profile feedback loop wired end-to-end (runtime → JSON → recompile)

title: formal: Lean lower-bound proofs for core runtime operations
description:
## Motivation

Formal lower bound proofs were used to demonstrate optimality (kernel cannot run in fewer than 1,081 cycles — proven from load engine capacity). Molt should prove lower bounds on core operations to know when we've hit theoretical limits.

## Current State

- 33 Lean 4 source files covering TIR semantics, pass correctness, NaN-boxing, refcount
- Correctness proofs for const-fold, DCE, SCCP, CSE, LICM
- No lower-bound or complexity proofs
- No connection between formal models and benchmark targets

## Proposed Changes

1. **String hash lower bound**: prove minimum cycles for hash computation given memory bandwidth and SIMD width constraints
2. **Dict probe lower bound**: prove expected probe count lower bound for open-addressing with given load factor
3. **Refcount lower bound**: prove minimum inc_ref/dec_ref operations per function call under Molt's ownership model
4. **Compilation complexity bounds**: prove that each mid-end pass is O(n) or O(n log n) in IR size, no worse
5. **Optimality certificates**: when a benchmark reaches within 5% of the proven lower bound, emit a certificate

## TODO(formal, owner:compiler, milestone:M3, priority:P2, status:planned): add Lean lower-bound theorems for hash, dict-probe, and refcount operations

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] At least 3 lower-bound theorems proven in Lean 4
- [ ] Each theorem maps to a concrete benchmark with measured vs. theoretical gap
- [ ] `lake build` succeeds with all proofs
- [ ] Optimality gap documented in ROADMAP.md

title: runtime: configuration space sweep infrastructure for automated optimization
description:
## Motivation

250K+ parameter configurations were swept to find 2-cycle improvements. Molt needs automated infrastructure to sweep optimization parameters (regalloc algorithm, SIMD thresholds, cache sizes, tier boundaries, pass budgets).

## Current State

- Manual tuning of Cranelift settings in `molt-backend/src/lib.rs`
- Hardcoded thresholds: SIMD min lengths (64 bytes), UTF8 cache shard count (8), tier cost boundaries (32KB/65KB)
- Benchmark suite exists (47 native benchmarks) but no automated sweep harness
- Profile JSON emission landed but not consumed by an optimizer

## Proposed Changes

1. **Parameter manifest**: TOML file listing all tunable parameters with ranges and types (int, float, enum, bool)
2. **Sweep runner**: `tools/sweep.py` — generates parameter combinations (grid, random, Bayesian), runs benchmark suite for each, records results
3. **Bayesian optimization mode**: use optuna or similar to intelligently explore the parameter space (not just grid search)
4. **Regression guard**: any configuration change must not regress any benchmark by >2% (statistical significance via bootstrap CI)
5. **Auto-apply**: best configuration written to `molt_tuning.toml` and loaded at compile time
6. **Agent-swarm integration**: Symphony agents can each explore a parameter subspace in parallel, reporting results to shared ledger

## TODO(perf, owner:tooling, milestone:M2, priority:P1, status:planned): build automated parameter sweep infrastructure with Bayesian optimization

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Parameter manifest covers >20 tunable parameters
- [ ] Sweep runner can evaluate 1000 configurations in <2 hours on benchmark kernel
- [ ] At least one parameter improvement discovered via sweep that manual tuning missed
- [ ] Symphony swarm can parallelize sweep across 5+ agents

title: moltlib: Python DSL kernel compilation for numeric/data workloads (Triton-inspired)
description:
## Motivation

The majority of GPU cycles will execute code written in Python DSLs that lower to machine code without going through C++ (Triton, JAX/Pallas, torch.compile, cuTile). Molt is uniquely positioned to be this for general Python — a Python DSL that compiles to native code. This issue tracks making the kernel compilation path explicit and optimized for numeric workloads.

## Current State

- Molt compiles full Python programs to native/WASM
- GPU architecture decision made (Arrow-first + libcudf, TIR kernel subset)
- No explicit kernel annotation or DSL subset for hot numeric code
- No vectorization hints or tile-based scheduling

## Proposed Changes

1. **`@molt.kernel` decorator**: annotate functions for aggressive optimization (no dynamic features, full type inference, SIMD vectorization)
2. **Kernel type system**: restricted type set (int, float, ndarray-like, tuple) with guaranteed unboxing
3. **Auto-vectorization**: loop bodies in kernel functions vectorized via SIMD where provably safe
4. **Tile scheduling**: for nested loops over arrays, automatic tiling with configurable tile sizes
5. **Kernel fusion**: adjacent kernel calls fused into single pass over data (operator fusion)
6. **GPU lowering path**: kernel IR → NVPTX via MLIR (future phase, after CPU kernelization)
7. **moltlib API**: `import moltlib; moltlib.kernel(fn)` — distinct from `libmolt` C-API

## TODO(runtime, owner:compiler, milestone:M3, priority:P1, status:planned): implement @molt.kernel decorator and kernel type system

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] @molt.kernel compiles annotated functions with full unboxing
- [ ] Auto-vectorized kernel is >5x faster than generic Molt compilation for numeric loops
- [ ] Kernel fusion works for 2+ adjacent calls
- [ ] moltlib importable as a Molt-native module

title: compiler: deopt-driven tiered recompilation with runtime profile feedback
description:
## Motivation

The 134x speedup came from reasoning about problem structure and proving bounds — not just instruction selection. Molt should use runtime execution data to drive recompilation decisions, creating a feedback loop between runtime profiling and compiler optimization.

## Current State

- Runtime emits profile JSON (call_bind IC hit/miss, dict prehash deopt, string split lane selection)
- Profile data not consumed by compiler for recompilation
- No tiered compilation (all functions compiled at same optimization level within tier)
- Guard failures logged but not acted upon

## Proposed Changes

1. **Profile ingestion**: compiler reads `molt_runtime_feedback.json` on recompilation and maps counters to IR functions
2. **Hot function promotion**: functions with high call counts or IC miss rates promoted to higher optimization tier
3. **Guard specialization**: guard failures trigger type-specialized recompilation (e.g., guard_tag mismatches → generate polymorphic dispatch)
4. **Deopt counters**: track per-guard deopt frequency; after threshold, trigger recompilation with relaxed type assumptions
5. **Speculative inlining**: inline callee at hot call sites based on IC hit rate (inline the common case, keep slow path)
6. **Multi-version functions**: emit 2 versions — speculative (fast, guarded) and generic (slow, safe) — with runtime dispatch

## TODO(compiler, owner:compiler, milestone:M3, priority:P1, status:planned): implement profile-guided recompilation pipeline

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Profile feedback loop: run → profile → recompile → faster run
- [ ] Hot function promotion shows measurable speedup on word_count and taq benchmarks
- [ ] Guard specialization reduces deopt rate by >50%
- [ ] No correctness regressions (speculative path must match generic path)
